_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Sangamon County, IL. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses that shape what genealogists can and cannot find.

current_courthouse.notes: The 1876 courthouse served for decades; Sangamon County offices now occupy adjacent county complex buildings. Springfield is also the state capital, placing the Illinois State Archives and Abraham Lincoln Presidential Library within walking distance of county records.

previous_courthouses.location: Springfield, original log courthouse near the public square
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced as Springfield grew. Abraham Lincoln was not yet in Sangamon County during this courthouse's tenure. Early records transferred.

previous_courthouses.location: Springfield, brick courthouse on the public square
previous_courthouses.fate: This is the courthouse where Abraham Lincoln tried cases as a young circuit-riding attorney beginning in 1837. Records survived transfer to the successor building.
Replaced by the current courthouse. Lincoln continued to practice here before his presidency. Records transferred intact.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or catastrophic loss
record_losses.description: Sangamon County has experienced no major courthouse fires or record losses. As the state capital county, its records have benefited from proximity to the Illinois State Archives and the general attention given to government facilities in Springfield. The county's records run continuously from its 1821 formation, making it one of the most complete documentary sets in central Illinois.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Sangamon County's unbroken record series means that if {ancestor_name} bought land, married, proved a will, or appeared in court here after 1821, the records almost certainly survive. Any gaps in the paper trail are more likely due to the ancestor's economic circumstances — tenancy rather than ownership, informal arrangements — than to record destruction.
record_losses.source: Power, John Carroll. History of the Early Settlers of Sangamon County, Illinois. Springfield: Edwin A. Wilson, 1876.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from county organization
deeds.gaps: No known gaps. Early deed books are well-preserved. The Illinois Central Railroad land grants (1850s–1860s) generated a surge of land transactions that are fully documented.
deeds.repository: Sangamon County Recorder, Springfield; Illinois State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com IL land records; Illinois State Archives digital collections

wills_estates.completeness: Excellent — probate records from county organization
wills_estates.gaps: Estate inventories from the 1820s and 1830s are sparse but present. Bound will books and probate registers are intact.
wills_estates.repository: Sangamon County Probate Court, Springfield; Illinois State Archives
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; limited Illinois State Archives digitization

marriages.completeness: Excellent — marriage licenses from county formation. Illinois required marriage licenses from 1836, but Sangamon County recorded marriages from 1821.
marriages.gaps: Pre-1836 records are marriage bonds and returns rather than licenses. Some gaps in earliest years.
marriages.repository: Sangamon County Clerk, Springfield; Illinois State Archives microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Good to excellent — circuit court minutes survive from organization. These include Abraham Lincoln's case records from the 1830s–1850s.
court_minutes.gaps: Justice of the peace records are incomplete for early decades. Circuit court records are robust.
court_minutes.repository: Sangamon County Circuit Court; Illinois State Archives; Abraham Lincoln Presidential Library
court_minutes.online_access: Lincoln Legal Papers project (digital); Illinois State Archives microfilm

vital_records.completeness: Illinois required county-level birth and death registration from 1877, but compliance was inconsistent until statewide registration in 1916. Springfield city records may be more complete than rural township records before 1916.
vital_records.repository: Sangamon County Clerk; Illinois Department of Public Health; Illinois State Archives

substitute_sources.source_type: Illinois State Archives — county records microfilm
substitute_sources.description: As the state capital county, Sangamon County's records have been extensively microfilmed by the Illinois State Archives. Original county records and ISA microfilm copies are often in the same city, providing redundancy.
substitute_sources.repository: Illinois State Archives, Springfield; Illinois Regional Archives Depository (IRAD) at UIS
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The ISA microfilm collection provides a critical backup for Sangamon County records and is accessible to researchers who cannot visit the courthouse. The IRAD system at the University of Illinois Springfield holds additional county records that did not remain in the courthouse.
substitute_sources.source: Illinois State Archives. Descriptive Inventory of the Archives of the State of Illinois. 2nd ed. Springfield: Office of the Secretary of State, 1978.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1820–1940 (1810 census lists Sangamon County area under Madison or Bond counties). Agricultural schedules (1850–1880) and manufacturing schedules (1820–1880) provide economic detail.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families. Agricultural schedules reveal the scale of each family's farming operation on the central Illinois prairie. The 1850 and 1860 censuses are critical for documenting Upper South migrant families who arrived in the 1830s–1840s.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Sangamon County, IL.

substitute_sources.source_type: Abraham Lincoln Presidential Library — local history collections
substitute_sources.description: The ALPL (formerly Illinois State Historical Library) holds extensive Sangamon County manuscript collections, including personal papers, business records, and organizational records from the 19th and 20th centuries.
substitute_sources.repository: Abraham Lincoln Presidential Library, Springfield, IL
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Personal papers and business records can document families between census years. The Lincoln Legal Papers project has transcribed and indexed thousands of case files from Sangamon County's circuit court, making this material accessible for any family involved in litigation during Lincoln's practice years.
substitute_sources.source: Abraham Lincoln Presidential Library. Manuscript Collections Finding Aids.

substitute_sources.source_type: Church records — Baptist, Methodist, Presbyterian, Christian Church (Disciples)
substitute_sources.description: Upper South migrants brought Baptist and Methodist traditions to Sangamon County. Presbyterian and Disciples of Christ congregations also formed early. Church records supplement civil records, particularly for vital events before 1877.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual congregations; Illinois State Archives; Sangamon Valley Collection at Lincoln Library (Springfield Public Library); denominational archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Baptism, marriage, and burial records from Sangamon County churches can fill gaps before civil registration in 1877. Church membership rolls and transfer letters document migration between congregations and track families moving within the county or to other counties.
substitute_sources.source: Bateman, Newton, and Paul Selby. Historical Encyclopedia of Illinois and History of Sangamon County. 2 vols. Chicago: Munsell, 1912.

narrative_hooks: Sangamon County's records run unbroken from 1821 — no fires, no floods, no war damage — and the Illinois State Archives sits less than a mile from the courthouse, which means if {ancestor_name} left a paper trail in this county, it almost certainly survives.

If {ancestor_name} appeared in Sangamon County's circuit court between 1837 and 1860, {pronoun_possessive} case may have been tried by Abraham Lincoln — and the Lincoln Legal Papers project may have transcribed the file.

Before 1877, Illinois did not require birth or death registration, so the absence of a birth certificate for {ancestor_name} born in Sangamon County reflects the era — church records, family Bibles, and census entries are the likeliest substitutes.

The agricultural schedules for Sangamon County (1850–1880) can reveal exactly what {ancestor_name}'s farm produced, how many acres {pronoun_subject} worked, and the value of {pronoun_possessive} livestock — putting the family's economic position in the context of the central Illinois prairie.

If {ancestor_name} arrived in Sangamon County from Kentucky, Virginia, or North Carolina in the 1830s or 1840s, the deed records may show {pronoun_object} purchasing land that had been recently opened by the displacement of the Kickapoo and Potawatomi — the paper trail begins at the county recorder, but the story begins earlier.
source_references: Power, John Carroll. History of the Early Settlers of Sangamon County, Illinois. Springfield: Edwin A. Wilson, 1876.
Bateman, Newton, and Paul Selby. Historical Encyclopedia of Illinois and History of Sangamon County. 2 vols. Chicago: Munsell, 1912.
Illinois State Archives. Descriptive Inventory of the Archives of the State of Illinois. 2nd ed. Springfield: Office of the Secretary of State, 1978.
Abraham Lincoln Presidential Library. Manuscript Collections Finding Aids.
National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Sangamon County, IL.
